pmos

User Acquisition — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Growth. User acquisition is the inflow stage of the growth system — the top of the funnel, distinct from activation, engagement, retention, and monetization, which all act on users acquisition has already brought in. The canon's organizing insight, from Gabriel Weinberg and Justin Mares' Traction, is that distribution — not product — is the modal cause of startup death, and that the way out is to test channels with the same rigor you test the product. Two practitioner frameworks dominate channel selection: Weinberg's Bullseye (19 channels, three rings, converging on the single channel that works) and Brian Balfour's product-channel fit (products are built to fit channels, not the reverse), nested inside his broader Four Fits model. The economics are governed by LTV:CAC (David Skok: above 3, payback under 12 months) with its known caveats. The compounding channels — content, virality, and growth loops — are contrasted with the decaying ones, governed by Andrew Chen's Law of Shitty Clickthroughs. And the canon insists the earliest acquisition is manual and unscalable, radiating outward through circles of trust. Where the field disagrees — funnels vs loops, chasing K>1 vs realistic sub-viral multipliers, channel focus vs diversification — this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 15 verified sources.

What user acquisition actually is

User acquisition is the work of getting strangers to become users — the inflow valve of the entire growth system. It sits at the top of the funnel, upstream of activation (first value), engagement (habitual use), retention (not leaving), and monetization (converting value to revenue). Every other growth stage operates on people acquisition has already delivered; acquisition is the only stage whose job is to manufacture the raw input the rest of the machine refines. That position makes it both the most visible growth lever and the one most prone to being conflated with growth itself.

The conflation is worth resisting. A team that pours money into acquisition while activation leaks, engagement is shallow, and retention is poor is filling a bucket with holes in it — and the canon is unanimous that the holes matter more than the tap. But the inverse failure is just as common and less discussed: a genuinely good product with no distribution. The single most cited line in the acquisition literature comes from Weinberg and Mares' Traction, summarized as the blunt observation that "Almost every failed startup has a product. What failed startups don't have is enough customers." Acquisition is the discipline of not being that startup.

There is a structural point hiding inside that quote. Most founders and PMs are, by background, product people — they are biased toward believing that if the product is good enough, distribution takes care of itself. The canon treats this as the central cognitive error of the field. Weinberg's Bullseye work is explicit that "Poor distribution — not product — is the number one cause of failure." Acquisition, properly understood, is not a marketing afterthought bolted on once the product is done; it is a co-equal discipline that deserves engineering-grade experimentation. Traction's recommendation makes the parity literal: "spending 50% of your time on product and 50% on traction" from the very beginning, rather than sequencing traction after product-market fit.

For a senior PM, the practical framing is this: acquisition is a portfolio of channels, each with its own physics — its own cost curve, its own saturation point, its own fit with your product and business model. The job is not "do marketing." The job is to discover which one or two channels can actually move your specific product at scale, to instrument their economics honestly, and to recognize that the answer changes over time as channels saturate. The rest of this primer walks the canon's map of that terrain.

The channel landscape and the Bullseye framework

The foundational taxonomy of acquisition channels comes from Traction, which enumerates nineteen distinct traction channels — from viral marketing, public relations, and search engine marketing through to offline ads, trade shows, and community building. The specific list matters less than its function: it is a forcing device against the natural tendency to reach for the two or three channels you already know. Weinberg and Mares' core behavioral claim is that founders systematically under-explore the channel space, defaulting to whatever is familiar, and that this is precisely how good products end up with no customers.

Their answer is the Bullseye framework, which structures channel selection as three concentric rings. As Weinberg describes it, the outer ring is what's possible: you brainstorm at least one reasonable idea for every one of the nineteen channels, suspending judgment about which will work. The middle ring is what's probable: you take the handful of channels that seem most promising and run cheap, fast, parallel tests to get real data rather than opinions. The inner ring is what's working: you identify the single channel that is actually moving the needle and pour resources into it. The name is the strategy — "you're aiming for the bullseye — the one traction channel at the center."

Two design choices in Bullseye are worth dwelling on because they are the ones PMs most often get wrong. First, the parallel testing in the middle ring is deliberately cheap and concurrent rather than sequential and thorough. The goal is a directional read on which channels can plausibly work for your product, not a polished campaign — running five small tests at once beats running one big test five times. Second, the convergence on a single channel is not a temporary expedient; it reflects an empirical regularity that recurs across the canon. Most companies, at any given moment, get the overwhelming majority of their growth from one channel, not a balanced blend. The discipline is to find that channel and resist the urge to dabble in the other eighteen.

The ReadinGraphics synthesis of Traction adds a competitive dimension that the bare framework can obscure: "You can get a competitive advantage by acquiring customers in ways your competitor isn't." Channels are partly a function of crowding. The reason the familiar channels are familiar is that everyone uses them, which is also why they are expensive and saturated. The Bullseye process, by forcing you through all nineteen, occasionally surfaces a channel your competitors have ignored — and an under-exploited channel is worth more than a crowded one of equal raw reach. This is the through-line connecting Bullseye to the saturation dynamics covered later: channel choice is not a one-time decision against a static landscape but a continuous search against a landscape that other people are also picking over.

Further reading: hbr.org — Harvard Business Review analysis of Apple's organizational structure: a functional model where experts lead experts, enabling innovation at scale, as Apple grew from $7B to $260B in revenue under this leadership model.

Product-channel fit

If Bullseye answers "how do I search the channel space," Brian Balfour's product-channel fit answers "why does a given channel work or fail for my specific product" — and his answer overturns the intuition most PMs start with. The instinct is to pick a product strategy first and then figure out how to market it. Balfour's central, repeated claim inverts the causality: "Products are built to fit with channels. Channels do not mold to products." The channel is the fixed constraint; the product is the variable you adapt to it.

The reason is leverage and control. As Balfour puts it, "You control your product, you do not control the channel." You cannot rewrite how a search engine ranks results, how a social feed's algorithm distributes content, how virally a referral mechanic spreads, or what a paid auction charges. Those are exogenous. What you can change is your product — its price point, its onboarding length, its shareability, its content surface area — so that it fits the mechanics of the channel you intend to ride. A product designed without a channel in mind is a product that will, by default, fit no channel well.

This reframes a lot of product decisions as covert channel decisions. A high-touch enterprise sales motion and a self-serve viral product are not just different go-to-market choices; they imply different products. A product priced at $20/month cannot support an outbound sales team, so it must be acquired through low-cost channels — which in turn dictates that the product be simple enough to sell itself. A product that depends on SEO must generate content or user-generated pages at scale, which is a product feature, not a marketing tactic. Balfour's point is that these couplings are not optional; they are the difference between a product that grows and one that doesn't.

The empirical signature of product-channel fit is concentration. Balfour observes that "at a given moment in time a company that has product channel fit will get 70%+ of their growth from one channel." This is the same regularity Bullseye converges toward, stated as a diagnostic: if your growth is spread evenly across many channels, that is not a sign of diversified strength but a likely sign that you have not actually found product-channel fit on any of them. Concentration is the fingerprint of fit. The corollary, which Balfour stresses elsewhere, is that this fit is time-bound — the channel that delivers 70% today can erode, forcing a product adaptation to a new channel, which is one of the hardest transitions a growth team faces precisely because it requires changing the product, not just the marketing.

Further reading: blog.hubspot.com — Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

The Four Fits: where channel sits in the system

Product-channel fit is one node in Balfour's larger Four Fits framework, which is his attempt to explain why some companies stall at $5M and others reach $100M+. The four fits are Market-Product, Product-Channel, Channel-Model, and Model-Market — and the framework's whole argument is that they are not independent levers you optimize one at a time. As Balfour writes, "you can't think about the four Fits in isolation because together they form an ecosystem for growth." You need all four, and they constrain each other.

The fit that acquisition PMs most often miss is Channel-Model — the coupling between how you acquire and how you make money. Balfour's formulation is that "Your channels are determined by your model and vice versa." A low-ARPU, high-volume business model can only sustain low-cost, high-throughput channels; it cannot afford a sales team or expensive paid acquisition. A high-ARPU enterprise model can afford — indeed requires — sales and account-based motions, but cannot rely on viral or content channels that deliver volume without the contract sizes to justify the touch. The model sets the unit economics that determine which channels are even viable, and the available channels constrain which models can scale.

Balfour uses a memorable contrast between two archetypes that fall out of these couplings: companies whose fits all reinforce each other grow smoothly, while companies fighting against their fits have to grind. The lesson for a PM is diagnostic rather than prescriptive. When acquisition isn't working, the instinct is to optimize the channel — better creative, more spend, tighter targeting. The Four Fits suggests the problem is often one layer up: the channel is fine but mismatched to the model, or the product wasn't built to fit the channel in the first place. The goal, as Balfour frames the endpoint, is that "You need all four Fits to grow to $100M+" — and a missing fit upstream caps growth no matter how well you execute the channel itself.

Paid acquisition and its decay

Paid acquisition — performance marketing across search, social, and display — is the channel that most resembles a vending machine: put money in, get users out, with a measurable cost per acquisition. That legibility is its great virtue and the source of its central illusion. The illusion is that paid is a stable, ownable channel. It is not. Andrew Chen's Law of Shitty Clickthroughs is the canonical statement of why: "Over time, all marketing strategies result in shitty clickthrough rates."

The data Chen marshals is stark. The first banner ad on HotWired in 1994 had a clickthrough rate of, in his telling, "HotWired CTR, 1994: 78%" — and by 2011 a typical Facebook ad clickthrough rate had fallen to roughly "Facebook CTR, 2011: 0.05%", a decline of more than three orders of magnitude. The mechanism is behavioral, not technological: "Customers respond to novelty, which inevitably fades." A new channel or ad format works precisely because it is new; as users habituate and competitors flood in, response rates collapse toward a floor. The early adopters of any paid channel earn outsized returns; the late majority pays saturated prices for exhausted attention.

This has a direct operational consequence for how PMs should think about paid. The returns on a paid channel are highest at the moment of its discovery and decay monotonically thereafter. The teams that win on paid are not the ones who optimize a mature channel to the third decimal place; they are the ones who find new channels, new formats, and new placements before the crowd. Chen's later writing makes the macro version of this point — competition on the major ad platforms intensifies rather than eases over time, with "competition is getting fiercer on Facebook ads, not less, which is evidenced by the rapid increase in the advertiser count". The advertiser count goes up, the auction prices go up, the clickthrough rates go down. Paid is a treadmill that speeds up.

None of this means paid is a bad channel; it means paid is a perishable one, and its perishability must be priced into the plan. A paid channel that pencils out today at a comfortable margin should be modeled with the assumption that its CAC will rise and its CTR will fall. The honest question is not "can I make paid work" but "can I make paid work fast enough, and at enough scale, before the channel saturates against me" — which routes directly into the unit economics that decide whether any paid channel is worth running at all.

Unit economics: CAC, LTV, and the ratio that governs them

Every acquisition channel ultimately answers to one question, which David Skok states plainly: "Can I make more profit from my customers than it costs me to acquire them?" The two quantities are lifetime value (the total gross margin a customer generates before churning) and customer acquisition cost (the fully-loaded cost to acquire them). The relationship between them, the LTV:CAC ratio, is the single most important number in acquisition economics — and the canon has settled on durable rules of thumb for it.

Skok's benchmark is the most cited: "The best SaaS businesses have a LTV to CAC ratio that is higher than 3, sometimes as high as 7 or 8." Below roughly 3, the business is spending too much to acquire relative to what it earns back, and growth tends to consume cash faster than it generates it. But the ratio alone is insufficient, because it says nothing about timing. A 5:1 ratio that takes three years to materialize can still bankrupt a company that has to fund the acquisition today. Skok's second guardrail addresses this: "the profitability is anemic if the time to recover CAC extends beyond 12 months." Payback period and ratio are two separate gates, and a channel must clear both.

Dave Kellogg's treatment of the same metric adds a conceptual anchor that connects acquisition back to monetization. His framing of why the ratio matters at all is a pricing principle: "What you pay for something should be a function of what it's worth." CAC is what you pay for a customer; LTV is what that customer is worth; the ratio is just that principle applied. He endorses the same threshold — "The general rule of thumb is that LTV/CAC should be 3.0 or higher, with of course, the higher the better."

But Kellogg is also the canon's most useful skeptic about the metric, and this is where a senior PM earns their keep. His caution is that LTV is computed from churn, and churn is a lagging, backward-looking input. In his vivid image, "today's churn rate is a function of yesterday's deals... like light arriving from a star that's three light-years away." The churn you measure today reflects the cohorts you acquired and the product you shipped quarters ago; using it to compute LTV and then to justify today's acquisition spend embeds a stale assumption at the center of the most important decision you make. A fast-growing company with improving retention will systematically understate true LTV; a deteriorating one will overstate it. The number is indispensable and the number is unreliable, and holding both of those facts at once is the actual skill. This is a contested-enough area — how to compute LTV, whether to use it at all for early-stage companies, how to handle expansion revenue — that any acquisition plan built on a single LTV:CAC figure without sensitivity analysis is overconfident.

Further reading: en.wikipedia.org — Wikipedia article on 'Nudge' by Thaler and Sunstein, the behavioral economics book introducing libertarian paternalism and the use of choice architecture to steer better decisions.

Organic and content as a compounding channel

Where paid decays, content compounds — and that asymmetry is the entire strategic case for organic channels. A paid campaign stops delivering the moment you stop paying; the spend and the result are coupled in real time. A piece of content, by contrast, keeps earning discovery long after it is published. The framing that captures this is the content flywheel: "The content flywheel model is a strategy where each piece of content you create builds on the success of the previous ones, fueling growth and momentum."

The flywheel metaphor is doing real work, not just decoration. A flywheel stores energy: each turn makes the next turn easier. In content terms, an accumulating library of assets builds domain authority, internal linking, and search ranking that lift every subsequent piece. The hundredth article ranks faster and reaches further than the first, because it inherits the authority the first ninety-nine built. This is why content is slow to start and hard to stop — the early returns are discouraging precisely because the flywheel has no momentum yet, and the late returns are formidable for the same reason in reverse. The strategic error PMs make with content is judging it on the timescale of paid, abandoning it in month three when the flywheel is just beginning to turn.

Content's compounding character also changes its competitive dynamics relative to paid. Paid is an auction you re-enter every day, so your competitors can outbid you tomorrow. A content moat, once built, is expensive for a competitor to replicate because they have to build their own flywheel from zero — they cannot simply outspend you into your accumulated authority. This is the organic counterpart to the Law of Shitty Clickthroughs: the value of paid attention decays toward a floor, while the value of an owned content asset accretes toward a ceiling set by the size of the addressable search and referral demand. The two channels have opposite time signatures, and a balanced acquisition portfolio often pairs a fast-but-decaying paid channel with a slow-but-compounding organic one.

The caveat is that content is not free — it is capital-intensive in labor rather than ad spend, and the flywheel only spins if the content is genuinely useful enough to earn links and rankings. The graveyard of content marketing is full of SEO-bait that never compounded because no one wanted to read it or link to it. Content as an acquisition channel is a bet that you can produce assets good enough to earn distribution on their merits, sustained long enough for the flywheel to catch. When that bet pays off, it produces the cheapest large-scale acquisition in the canon; when it doesn't, it is a slow, expensive way to produce a library no one reads.

Virality and the k-factor

Virality is the channel where the product acquires users on the company's behalf — where existing users bring in new ones through invitations, shares, or sheer visible usage. Its governing metric is the viral coefficient, or k-factor, defined cleanly by First Round Review: "K = (average number of invites sent by each user) × (conversion rate of invitees)." The k-factor is the average number of new users each user generates, and its value relative to 1 determines the entire character of the channel.

When K exceeds 1, the dynamics are explosive: "Every user creates more users, leading to compounding growth." Each cohort more than replaces itself, and growth becomes self-sustaining without further acquisition spend. This is the dream that launched a thousand referral programs — and it is, in practice, vanishingly rare and almost never durable. The honest quantification comes from the getlaunchlist analysis, which reports that in real waitlists and products "K = 0.3–0.7, which still meaningfully compounds when paired with a fast viral cycle time." Sustained K>1 is the exception that proves the rule; most products that are "viral" in the colloquial sense operate well below 1.

The crucial reframe — and the one that separates sophisticated growth thinking from referral-program cargo-culting — is that sub-viral K is still enormously valuable as a multiplier on other acquisition, not as a standalone engine. The getlaunchlist piece makes the arithmetic concrete: "A K of 0.5 means every 1,000 signups you acquire eventually becomes ~2,000 signups (1,000 + 500 + 250 + 125 + …)". A k-factor of 0.5 doubles the yield of every paid or organic user you bring in — it does not replace acquisition, it amplifies it. A PM who measures K and finds 0.4 should not conclude "we're not viral, scrap the referral mechanic"; they should conclude "every dollar of acquisition spend now buys 1.67× the users it used to," which is one of the highest-leverage improvements available.

Two cautions complete the picture. First, virality decays as you saturate the network: "None of them sustained K > 1 forever. K decays as you saturate the addressable network." The pool of un-invited people in any user's network is finite, so the same invitation mechanic that worked at small scale weakens as the addressable graph fills in. Second, and most important, First Round's own caveat: "A good virality score doesn't guarantee sustainability." A high K that brings in users who don't activate or retain inflates the top of the funnel while the bottom leaks — virality without retention is a way to acquire churn at scale. The k-factor is a measure of inflow, and inflow only matters if the rest of the system holds it.

Growth loops vs funnels

The most important conceptual shift in modern acquisition thinking is the move from funnels to loops, and it is also the canon's most active live debate. The traditional funnel is linear: you pour acquisition in the top, users fall through awareness, consideration, and conversion, and some fraction emerges as customers. The funnel is a one-way pipe, and its defining weakness is that the output does not feed the input — every new cohort requires fresh acquisition effort poured in at the top.

Growth loops invert this. As the Ortto explainer defines them, "Growth Loops are closed systems designed to ensure every input goes through a series of pre-defined steps to generate an output." The defining property is that the output is reinvested as the next input: "Growth loops are created so that one cohort of users leads to another cohort of users, meaning you are reinvesting the output." Where a funnel leaks, a loop compounds — because the users it produces become the fuel for producing more users.

Ortto catalogs the loop types, and they map neatly onto channels already covered. Viral loops are the k-factor mechanism: users invite users. User-generated-content loops produce content (reviews, listings, profiles, answers) that ranks in search and pulls in new users who then create more content — the engine behind marketplaces and community products. Paid loops close the economic circle: revenue from acquired users funds the acquisition of more users, which only works when the unit economics clear. Each loop is a different way of wiring the output back to the input, and the strongest growth companies typically run one dominant loop rather than a balanced set.

Here the live debate must be flagged honestly, because the field genuinely disagrees and a senior PM will encounter both camps. The loops-first school (most associated with Reforge-adjacent practitioners) argues that funnel thinking is fundamentally outdated — that treating acquisition as a one-way pipe causes teams to chase top-of-funnel volume while ignoring the reinvestment mechanics that actually compound. The funnel-defenders counter that loops are an elegant abstraction that obscures the concrete, stage-by-stage conversion work most teams still need to do, and that not every business has a natural loop. The defensible position is that loops and funnels are complementary lenses: the loop tells you whether your growth can compound without ever-increasing input, and the funnel tells you where, mechanically, users are falling out of whichever steps the loop comprises. Neither fully replaces the other, and a team that abandons funnel instrumentation in the name of loops often loses the ability to diagnose where its loop is leaking.

Further reading: newsletter.aroundthebonfire.com — Kevan Lee's Substack post explaining growth loops — self-reinforcing cycles that drive compounding user acquisition and retention, with frameworks for identifying and building them.

Doing things that don't scale

Before any channel, loop, or coefficient, there is the cold start — and the canon's most counterintuitive teaching is that the right way to begin acquisition is to do it by hand, unscalably, one user at a time. Paul Graham's essay is the definitive statement: "The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start is to recruit users manually." The instinct to build a scalable acquisition machine before you have any users is, in Graham's telling, a form of premature optimization that founders use to avoid the uncomfortable, low-status work of personally convincing strangers to try their product.

The active verb matters. Graham is emphatic that early acquisition is a push, not a pull: "You can't wait for users to come to you. You have to go out and get them." There is no organic discovery for a product no one has heard of, no virality without a base population to spread from, no content flywheel without authority. At zero, every one of the compounding channels has nothing to compound. The only thing available is direct, manual, person-to-person recruitment — emailing people you know, demoing to anyone who will sit still, hand-holding the first users through a product that is still rough.

Graham's deepest point is that manual acquisition is not merely a necessary evil to be endured until the scalable channels kick in; it is a load-bearing part of building a good product at all. In his framing, recruiting users by hand is "a necessary part of the feedback loop that makes the product good." The close contact with early users that manual acquisition forces — watching them struggle, hearing their objections, fixing what breaks in front of you — is the same contact that produces the product insight you cannot get from a dashboard. The unscalable phase is where product-market fit is actually found, which is why trying to skip it by jumping straight to scalable channels tends to scale a product that isn't yet good enough to be worth scaling. The transition to scalable channels is something you earn by first doing the unscalable thing well, not something you start with to avoid it.

First customers and the geometry of trust

The manual recruitment Graham prescribes has a structure, and Lenny Rachitsky's synthesis of how companies like Figma, Stripe, and Gong won their first customers maps it precisely. The organizing idea is that early acquisition radiates outward through concentric circles of decreasing trust, starting from the founder's own relationships. The first customers are not strangers reached through a channel; they are the people who already trust you, and then the people who trust them.

The mechanism is trust transfer, captured in a line Rachitsky quotes from Figma's Sho Kuwamoto: "It's all a matter of trust. Somebody I trust is going to say, you should use this tool." A cold prospect has no reason to believe an unknown startup's claims; a warm introduction from someone they already trust collapses the credibility gap that no amount of marketing can close at the cold-start stage. This is why the sequence reliably begins with former colleagues — people who already know whether your judgment is sound — rather than with the theoretically ideal customer profile.

Rachitsky's documented sequence is a practical ordering of the circles: start with personal networks (former colleagues first), then move to strategic cold outreach, then leverage investor introductions, then communities where your users gather, then content that earns inbound, then press, and only later the broad organic and paid channels. The ordering is not arbitrary — it tracks the trust gradient. Each ring is harder to convert than the one inside it because each is further from the founder's personal credibility, so you exhaust the high-trust inner rings before spending effort on the low-trust outer ones. The practical discipline is to resist the temptation to start at the outer rings — to launch on a public channel before you have wrung every customer out of your own network — because the outer rings convert poorly until the inner rings have produced the case studies, testimonials, and word-of-mouth that make the cold pitch credible.

This connects back to product-channel fit and the 70%-from-one-channel regularity. Even at the first-ten-customers stage, the canon observes that early traction tends to come from one or two channels, not a broad spread — and at this stage that channel is almost always the founder's network and its immediate extensions. The first scalable channel is discovered later; the first customers come from trust.

Further reading: uxdesign.cc — Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

Channel saturation and why growth gets harder

Every channel covered so far shares a property that deserves its own treatment because it is the single most under-modeled force in acquisition planning: channels saturate, and saturation is not a risk to be managed but a near-certainty to be planned around. The Law of Shitty Clickthroughs is the micro version — individual ad formats and placements decay as novelty fades. Andrew Chen's broader argument is the macro version: the entire acquisition environment gets harder over time, structurally and irreversibly.

Chen's diagnosis names three compounding forces. The first is platform competition: "competition is getting fiercer on Facebook ads, not less, which is evidenced by the rapid increase in the advertiser count" — more advertisers chasing the same finite attention bids prices up and returns down. The second is consumer adaptation: "everyone's getting smarter about growth, including consumers" — the tactics that worked when users were naïve to them stop working once users recognize and discount them. The third is the saturation of the channels themselves — the addressable audience in any given channel is finite, and as you and your competitors penetrate it, the marginal new user gets more expensive to reach.

The strategic implication is that no channel is a permanent asset, and an acquisition strategy that assumes today's working channel will keep working is planning to be surprised. The companies that sustain growth through saturation do so by treating channel discovery as a continuous process rather than a solved problem — diversifying into new channels, new markets, and new monetization surfaces before the current channel exhausts. This is the dynamic version of Bullseye: you converge on the one channel that works now, ride it hard, and simultaneously keep the Bullseye search running so that when the current channel decays — and it will — you have the next one identified rather than starting the search from a position of declining numbers. Saturation is also why the compounding channels (content, loops) are strategically prized: they decay more slowly than paid, and an owned content moat or a working growth loop is more defensible against the saturation that erodes purely paid strategies.

Choosing channels: focus over breadth

All the foregoing converges on a single decision a PM has to make: which channel, or channels, to actually invest in. The canon's answer is strikingly consistent and runs against most teams' instincts — focus narrowly, and resist the pull toward breadth. First Round's consumer-acquisition playbook makes the case most directly, simplifying the nineteen-channel landscape for consumer companies down to three viable lanes: "For consumer companies, there are only three growth 'lanes' that comprise the majority of new customer acquisition: Performance marketing, Virality, and Content."

The reduction from nineteen channels to three lanes is not a contradiction of Bullseye but its consumer-specific resolution — and the playbook's central warning is about the failure mode of breadth. The most common acquisition mistake, in First Round's framing, is "investing in too many channels at once, and as a result not investing in any one channel enough." Channels reward depth. Each has a learning curve, a creative practice, an instrumentation overhead, and a scale threshold below which it never really works. Spreading effort thin across many channels guarantees that none of them crosses the threshold where it actually compounds.

The encouraging and the sobering halves of the conclusion arrive together: "you generally only need to excel at one lane to build a successful business. The bad news is that it won't be easy." You do not need to win every channel; you need to win one. But winning one means depth, persistence, and the willingness to keep investing past the discouraging early returns — exactly the patience the content flywheel demands and exactly the focus the Bullseye inner ring prescribes.

Here, though, sits the field's other genuine tension, and a senior PM should hold it explicitly rather than pretending the canon speaks with one voice. The focus camp (First Round, Balfour's 70%-from-one-channel, Bullseye's single inner-ring channel) says concentrate. The saturation camp (Chen's law of shitty clickthroughs and growth-gets-hard thesis) says any single channel will decay, so you must diversify and keep searching. These are not actually contradictory once you add the time dimension: focus your investment on the one channel that works now, while continuously searching for the next one — concentration in execution, breadth in exploration. The teams that get this wrong fail in one of two ways: they diversify their investment prematurely and never get any channel to scale, or they concentrate so completely that they have no successor channel ready when their one channel saturates. The resolution is a portfolio not of equally-funded channels but of one funded channel and an ongoing, cheap exploration of candidates — which is, in the end, just Bullseye run as a permanent loop rather than a one-time exercise.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

User acquisition is the inflow stage; it hands off to and depends on several adjacent topics worth a deliberate next hop.

  • Activation — acquisition's immediate downstream. The users acquisition delivers are worthless until they reach first value; a high-K viral channel or a cheap paid channel that feeds a leaky activation step is acquiring churn. The First Round caveat that virality doesn't guarantee sustainability is really a pointer to activation and retention.
  • Retention — the denominator of LTV, and therefore the hidden governor of every CAC decision. Because LTV is computed from churn, retention quietly determines which acquisition channels are economically viable at all; Kellblog's "light from a distant star" caveat lives at this boundary.
  • Monetization and pricing — Channel-Model fit means your business model determines which channels you can afford, and the LTV in LTV:CAC is a monetization output. Acquisition economics and pricing are the same conversation viewed from opposite ends.
  • Product-market fit — the prerequisite Graham's unscalable phase is really about discovering. Acquisition before PMF scales a product that isn't ready; the manual-recruitment feedback loop is how PMF gets found in the first place.
  • Growth modeling and experimentation — the quantitative substrate beneath channel selection: building the loop math, sizing the funnel, and running the cheap parallel tests Bullseye's middle ring demands.
  • Marketplaces and network effects — where UGC growth loops and virality become the dominant acquisition mechanism rather than a multiplier, and where channel-product fit is most tightly coupled to the product's core mechanics.

References

  1. blog.hubspot.com ·cited· (T3)

    Explains enterprise sales and how it differs from SMB and mid-market sales across eight dimensions including deal length, number of decision makers, and purchase complexity, with guidance on building an enterprise sales strategy.

  2. hbr.org ·cited· (T3)

    Harvard Business Review analysis of Apple's organizational structure: a functional model where experts lead experts, enabling innovation at scale, as Apple grew from $7B to $260B in revenue under this leadership model.

  3. uxdesign.cc ·cited· (T3)

    Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

  4. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T3)

    Wikipedia article on 'Nudge' by Thaler and Sunstein, the behavioral economics book introducing libertarian paternalism and the use of choice architecture to steer better decisions.

  5. newsletter.aroundthebonfire.com (T3)

    Kevan Lee's Substack post explaining growth loops — self-reinforcing cycles that drive compounding user acquisition and retention, with frameworks for identifying and building them.

  6. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Brian Balfour's product-channel-fit thesis: "Products are built to fit with channels. Channels do not mold to products." "You control your product, you do not control the channel." And the power law: "at a given moment in time a company that has product channel fit will get 70%+ of their growth from one channel."

  7. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Balfour's Four Fits framework for $100M+ growth: Market-Product, Product-Channel, Channel-Model, Model-Market. "You need all four Fits to grow to $100M+... you can't think about the four Fits in isolation because together they form an ecosystem for growth." "Your channels are determined by your model and vice versa."

  8. medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Gabriel Weinberg's Bullseye framework (from Traction): 19 traction channels, narrowed through three rings — outer (what's possible, all 19), middle (what's probable, cheap tests on 2-3), inner (what's working, the one dominant channel). "Poor distribution — not product — is the number one cause of failure." "you're aiming for the bullseye — the one traction channel at the center."

  9. andrewchen.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Andrew Chen's Law of Shitty Clickthroughs: "Over time, all marketing strategies result in shitty clickthrough rates." Banner CTR fell from 78% (HotWired, 1994) to 0.05% (Facebook, 2011). Primary driver: "Customers respond to novelty, which inevitably fades." Every channel decays as users habituate and competitors crowd in.

  10. paulgraham.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Paul Graham, Do Things That Don't Scale: "The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start is to recruit users manually." "You can't wait for users to come to you. You have to go out and get them." Manual acquisition is "a necessary part of the feedback loop that makes the product good," not merely permissible.

  11. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Lenny Rachitsky on first 10 B2B customers (interviews w/ Figma, Stripe, Gong, Census): early acquisition is concentric circles of decreasing trust radiating from the founder, starting with former colleagues. "It's all a matter of trust. Somebody I trust is going to say, you should use this tool." Sequence: personal network → cold outbound → investor intros → communities → content → press → launch.

  12. review.firstround.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    First Round Review's K-factor definition: "K = (average number of invites sent by each user) × (conversion rate of invitees)." K>1 = "Every user creates more users, leading to compounding growth"; K<1 needs other acquisition to sustain. Caveat: "A good virality score doesn't guarantee sustainability" — virality without retention inflates then churns.

  13. forentrepreneurs.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    David Skok's SaaS unit-economics guardrails: "The best SaaS businesses have a LTV to CAC ratio that is higher than 3, sometimes as high as 7 or 8." And on CAC payback: "profitability is anemic if the time to recover CAC extends beyond 12 months." The governing question: "Can I make more profit from my customers than it costs me to acquire them?"

  14. andrewchen.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Andrew Chen on why growth is getting harder: channel saturation, intensifying ad-platform competition ("competition is getting fiercer on Facebook ads, not less"), and "everyone's getting smarter about growth, including consumers." As channels oversaturate, returns compress and teams must diversify or find new channels.

  15. getlaunchlist.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Viral-coefficient guide quantifying virality realistically: "most healthy waitlists land at K = 0.3–0.7," not K>1. Sub-viral still compounds: "A K of 0.5 means every 1,000 signups you acquire eventually becomes ~2,000 signups." And "None of them sustained K > 1 forever. K decays as you saturate the addressable network" — virality is a multiplier on other acquisition, not a standalone engine.

  16. review.firstround.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    First Round consumer-acquisition playbook: "For consumer companies, there are only three growth 'lanes' that comprise the majority of new customer acquisition: Performance marketing, Virality, and Content." The failure mode is "investing in too many channels at once, and as a result not investing in any one channel enough." "you generally only need to excel at one lane to build a successful business."

  17. ortto.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Growth-loops explainer: "Growth Loops are closed systems designed to ensure every input goes through a series of pre-defined steps to generate an output," with the output reinvested as new input — "one cohort of users leads to another cohort of users." Loop types: viral, user-generated-content (UGC drives search discovery), and paid (revenue funds more acquisition). Loops compound where funnels leak.

  18. kellblog.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Dave Kellogg on LTV/CAC as the ultimate SaaS metric: "What you pay for something should be a function of what it's worth"; rule of thumb "LTV/CAC should be 3.0 or higher." Key caveat — it is backward-looking: "today's churn rate is a function of yesterday's deals... like light arriving from a star that's three light-years away."

  19. omnius.so ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Content-flywheel framing: "The content flywheel model is a strategy where each piece of content you create builds on the success of the previous ones, fueling growth and momentum." Content is the acquisition channel that compounds — each asset keeps earning discovery over time rather than resetting when spend stops.

  20. readingraphics.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Traction (Weinberg & Mares) summary: "spending 50% of your time on product and 50% on traction"; "Almost every failed startup has a product. What failed startups don't have is enough customers"; and "You can get a competitive advantage by acquiring customers in ways your competitor isn't." 19 channels, Bullseye three rings, overcome bias toward familiar channels.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-15_user-acquisition.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
